food_subgroup: Fish curry dishes; braised or stewed fish head in spiced gravy with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'fish curry (food)' class (FOODON_03311737); no specific 'fish head curry' subclass exists. 'closest_parent' match type used as the best available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked, in sauce or gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers fish cooked in sauce/gravy; no dedicated fish head curry code exists. 'closest_parent' match type used as the best available anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for fish head curry identified; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: fish head (typically red snapper or grouper)
spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, garam masala)

serving.description: 1 portion (shared dish, approximately 400 g per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fish head curry in Singapore is approximately 400 g per person, comprising a large section of fish head with curry gravy and assorted vegetables such as okra and eggplant.

nutrient_source.title: Fish curry, with gravy — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fish curry components for sodium and fibre cross-check
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the closest approved primary source for North Indian fish curry. Values are adapted from the 'fish curry with gravy' entry (per 100 g edible portion). The dish served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore is closely analogous to this preparation. Sodium value cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 35193, Fish curry). Serving size and vegetable composition reflect Singapore restaurant norms. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for fish head curry was identified; ICMR-NIN used as primary regional source per source priority policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish curry dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Fish curry' and 'Curry dishes with low carbohydrate content' entries; University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fish head curry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with fish curry and low-carbohydrate spiced meat/fish dishes reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 44(5):e1-e13. These dishes consistently fall in the GI range 30–45. A GI of 35 (low category) is used as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 3.5 × 4.0) / 100 = 4.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per 400 g serving, approaching 73% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single dish)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute up to 1680 mg sodium, which substantially exceeds recommended single-meal sodium targets of 600–800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low net carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100 g) with high protein content (~10.2 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish head curry has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; however, sodium content should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish and anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish head curry provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids from the fish, but the high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from curry paste and cooking oil warrant portion control and reduced-frequency consumption for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Head Curry is a richly spiced dish consisting of a whole or halved fish head — most commonly red snapper (ang cho) or grouper — slow-stewed in a thick, aromatic curry gravy with assorted vegetables such as okra (lady's fingers), eggplant (brinjal), tomatoes, and green chillies. In Singapore, the dish is served across Indian, Chinese-Indian fusion, and North Indian restaurant contexts, and is considered a cultural icon of the island's multicultural food heritage. The North Indian restaurant variant, as served at establishments such as Bawarchi and Aryana on South Bridge Road, features a tomato-onion-based curry enriched with whole spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chilli, producing a deeply savoury, moderately spicy gravy. The fish head is prized for its gelatinous cheek meat, collar flesh, and eye socket fat, which contribute a silky, unctuous mouthfeel. Nutritionally, fish head curry is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content and a low glycemic index, making it suitable for blood sugar management when consumed without large portions of white rice. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. Key clinical considerations include its high sodium content from curry paste and added salt, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, B vitamins (particularly B12), phosphorus, and iron from the fish. A typical restaurant portion is approximately 400 g per person and is usually shared as part of a multi-dish meal.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for fish head curry was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN 'fish curry with gravy' entry (2017), which is the closest approved primary regional source for North Indian fish curry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium rather than low because ICMR-NIN is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and the preparation method is closely analogous.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), 'fish curry with gravy' entry, per 100 g edible portion. This is the closest approved primary source for North Indian fish curry as served in Singapore. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry was found. 2. Sodium value cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fish curry entry. The sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant preparation with curry paste and added salt; home-cooked versions may be lower. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for fish head curry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 3.5 × 4.0) / 100 = 4.9 g. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in fish and vegetable oil-based curry preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA data. 6. The dish is classified as North Indian / Singaporean cuisine; the three source restaurants (Bawarchi, Bombay Curry and Kebabs, Aryana) are all North Indian establishments on South Bridge Road, Singapore. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the North Indian Muslim restaurant context; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. Price range SGD 32.90–39.90 per dish (field survey data data, preserved from input). 9. FoodOn match FOODON_03311737 ('fish curry') is a closest_parent match; no specific fish head curry subclass exists in FoodOn as of April 2026. FoodEx2 A0CKP is a closest_parent match for fish cooked in sauce/gravy.
